summary: Continuous flow is an agile scheduling approach where teams prioritize user stories in a list and pull new work as they complete items, using WIP limits to stay focused on finishing rather than accumulating nearly-done work. It eliminates the overhead of iteration planning, estimation, and capacity calculation. However, the approach works best for experienced teams handling unpredictable work streams, while less experienced teams benefit from the feedback loops that timeboxed iterations provide. Teams using continuous flow should maintain enough slack to handle surges—a continuously busy team is a warning sign.
key_points: WIP limits prevent accumulation of 'nearly done' work and keep teams focused on completing stories
Continuous flow suits unpredictable work streams like bug fixing, but teams need slack capacity to handle surges
The approach eliminates iteration ceremony but loses the feedback loops that help spot problems like technical debt
Continuous flow works well for skilled teams but less experienced teams benefit from iteration structure
The method handles unpredictable arrival of work better than unpredictable nature of work
money_quotes: Once they reach the WIP limit, they can't start any more stories until they complete one already in progress.
WIP limits are important because they keep the team focused on finishing stories, otherwise it's too easy to build up a pile of 'nearly done' work.
If a continuous flow team is always busy, that's usually a red flag.
The regular cadence of iterations provide a feedback loop that helps a team spot problems such as cruft building up in the code base.
Continuous flow is effective for skilled teams that want to reduce the ceremony in their work, but less experienced teams are better off with iterations.
Continuous flow is good for the unpredictable arrival of work, but less so when the nature of the work is unpredictable or poorly understood.
key_insight: Continuous flow trades iteration overhead for autonomy, but that trade-off only pays off for experienced teams who don't need the guardrails that regular cadences provide.
